task: Check if the /analyze page works end-to-end: paste a sample package.json with express, prisma, jest, and tailwindcss into the analyzer and verify: (1) the health score renders, (2) migration intelligence shows for jest and express, (3) the page doesn't error. Test by POSTing to /api/analyze on production with a real manifest. Report what the user experience looks like.

assignments.backend: POST to https://indiestack.ai/api/analyze (or /api/analyze on production) with a real package.json manifest containing express, prisma, jest, and tailwindcss. Verify: (1) response returns 200 with a health score, (2) per-dependency data includes status and recommendations, (3) migration intelligence data is present for jest and express specifically, (4) no error responses. Report the full response structure.
assignments.frontend: Check the /analyze page on production (https://indiestack.ai/analyze): (1) does the page load without errors, (2) review the route file at src/indiestack/routes/ to understand what the page renders — health score display, dependency cards, migration insights section, (3) check if the page has proper error handling for empty/malformed input, (4) report what the end-to-end user experience looks like — is it clear, does it guide the user, are results well-formatted?

decomposition.reasoning: This is an end-to-end verification task with two distinct angles: (1) API correctness — does /api/analyze return proper data with health scores and migration intelligence, best tested by Backend via a real POST request; (2) Page UX — does /analyze render results clearly and handle edge cases, best assessed by Frontend reviewing both the live page and the route code. Both can run in parallel since they test different layers. No DevOps, Content, or MCP involvement needed — this is pure functional verification.

strategy_review.reasoning: This is a focused functional test of a feature we've never audited before (no /analyze task in the playbook). The analyze page is a direct demonstration of IndieStack's intelligence-layer value prop — if it's broken or ugly, it undermines the pitch. Cost should be ~$0.40-0.60 based on similar audit tasks (MCP audit was $0.31, /migrations check was $0.36). Two departments make sense here: backend verifies the API contract (does the data come back correctly), frontend verifies the rendered experience (does it look good to a human). Non-destructive, read-only, low risk.

approved_tasks.backend: POST to https://indiestack.ai/api/analyze (or /api/analyze on production) with a real package.json manifest containing express, prisma, jest, and tailwindcss. Verify: (1) response returns 200 with a health score, (2) per-dependency data includes status and recommendations, (3) migration intelligence data is present for jest and express specifically, (4) no error responses. Report the full response structure.
approved_tasks.frontend: Check the /analyze page on production (https://indiestack.ai/analyze): (1) does the page load without errors, (2) review the route file at src/indiestack/routes/ to understand what the page renders — health score display, dependency cards, migration insights section, (3) check if the page has proper error handling for empty/malformed input, (4) report what the end-to-end user experience looks like — is it clear, does it guide the user, are results well-formatted?

conditions: Neither department should make code changes — this is audit only, report findings
If the page or API is broken, report the exact error rather than attempting a fix
risk_flags: If /api/analyze doesn't exist or returns 404, the task ends quickly but that's a valid finding worth knowing
